steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.illi: 'illi' is a demonstrative pronoun/adjective meaning 'to that'
hints.principi: 'principi' is a noun meaning 'to the chief / ruler'
hints.acceptius: 'acceptius' is a comparative adjective meaning 'more acceptable'

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere' — 'est' here comes from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to go out: 'to go out' is 'exire' — do not confuse with 'esse'
hints.to stand: 'to stand' is 'stare' — 'est' is from the verb 'esse'

hints.Perfect indicative: Perfect of 'esse' would be 'fuit' — 'est' is present
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive of 'esse' would be 'sit' — 'est' is indicative
hints.Future indicative: Future of 'esse' would be 'erit' — 'est' is present

hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat) — 'est' here is from 'esse'
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand) — different verb
hints.exeo, exire, exii, exitum: These are the parts of 'exire' (to go out) — not 'esse'

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'princeps' — 'principi' is the dative singular
hints.Ablative: Ablative singular would be 'principe' — 'principi' ends in -i (dative)
hints.Genitive: Genitive singular would be 'principis' — 'principi' is dative

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'princeps, principis' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension dative would end in -o — 'principi' is 3rd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension dative would end in -ui — 'principi' is 3rd declension

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'Deus' — 'Deo' is dative/ablative form
hints.Ablative: While 'Deo' can be ablative, here it is in apposition to 'illi principi' (dative)
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'Deum' — 'Deo' is dative here

hints.1st: 1st declension dative would end in -ae — 'Deus, Dei' is 2nd declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension dative would end in -i — 'Deo' follows the 2nd declension -o pattern
hints.4th: 4th declension dative would end in -ui — 'Deo' is 2nd declension

hints.Positive neuter nominative: The positive form would be 'acceptum' — the -ius ending signals the comparative
hints.Superlative neuter nominative: The superlative would be 'acceptissimum' — '-ius' is the comparative ending
hints.Comparative masculine accusative: Masculine accusative comparative would be 'acceptiorem' — '-ius' is neuter nominative/accusative

steps.prompt: What does Cicero say is most pleasing to God in this passage?
steps.target: The assemblies and communities of people united by law
choices: The assemblies and communities of people united by law

hints.Military conquest and empire: In the Somnium Scipionis, Cicero emphasizes civic virtue, not military conquest, as most pleasing to God
hints.Sacrifices and temple offerings: Cicero refers to civic communities (concilia coetusque hominum), not ritual worship
hints.Individual philosophical contemplation: The context praises communal civic life, not solitary philosophy

translation: For nothing is more acceptable to that chief God.
